3: Over the last ten years, the global dairy herd increased by 11% and milk yield per cow increased by 17% (FAO, 2018). Milk pro- duction is projected to continue to increase as demand in countries like China grows (Bai et al., 2018). Cattle products are a valuable

source of nutrition and livelihood for millions of people around the world (FAO, 2018). Increasing milk demand has been met by higher yields per cow, increasing the number of cows or a combination of both, depending on the region of the world. Meanwhile, global beef production is also growing, though at a slower rate than milk production (FAOSTAT, 2019). Latin America is a major cattle pro- ducing region. It is home to 28% of the world's live cattle, producing 15% of milk and 25% of meat consumed globally (FAOSTAT, 2019). Recent studies have compared the environmental intensity of beef production in this region (Ruviaro et al., 2015; Dick et al., 2015;

Mazzetto et al., 2015; Florindo et al., 2017). However, few studies have compared the environmental intensity of milk production in Latin America, and as far as we are aware, none have compared the impact of combined beef and milk production in this region. The agrarian sector is an important component of the Costa Rican economy (INEC, 2017), within which dairy production is a major activity. In 2009, Costa Rica joined a free-trade agreement (DR- CAFTA - including the United States, Dominican Republic, El Sal- vador, Guatemala, Honduras and Nicaragua), which includes mea- sures to gradually reduce import tariffs on milk products that will strongly influence the internal market and could place financial strain on national milk producers (Lizano and Vega, 2016).

Meanwhile, in 2007 Costa Rica set itself the challenging target of becoming carbon neutral by 2021 (Flagg, 2018). These factors have led to an imperative towards sustainable intensification and specialisation in dairy production (more details on the Costa Rican dairy sector in Supplementary Material, section S1). Several studies have explored the effect of intensification on dairy production systems and concluded that intensification is an important strategy for mitigating the environmental burdens of milk production (Bakken et al., 2017; Chobtang et al., 2017; Gerssen-Gondelach et al., 2017). The intensification of dairy farms increases the milk yield per cow and could lead to a reduction in the number of ani- mals required to satisfy milk demand (Styles et al., 2018). A consequence of this intensification is that fewer calves and culled dairy cows would be sent for beef production. Any trend towards specialisation in milk production changes the herd structure, focusing on female animals and reducing the number of beef ani- mals raised or exported from a dairy farm. Consequently, the de- mand for beef would need to be met by increasing numbers of dedicated beef cattle (Baldini et al., 2017). Styles et al. (2018), Vellinga and de Vries (2018) and Soteriades et al. (2019) showed that the balance between milk and beef production could have environmental consequences, especially for GHG emissions and land occupation (LO).

Quantifying the impacts of dairy systems is a complex process. One method to account for environmental impacts is to undertake Life Cycle Assessment (LCA). Most LCA studies dealing with milk production are performed on a "cradle to farm-gate" basis (Baldini et al., 2017), and have shown that higher milk yields per cow lead to smaller environmental footprints, especially for global warming potential (GWP) (Gerber et al., 2011). However, those studies did not consider implications for coupled dairy-beef production and pure-beef production systems. This is particularly important because beef production has a higher environmental intensity than milk production, and 45% of beef produced globally is dairy-beef (i.e. beef output from dairy farms) (Opio et al., 2013). Due to diffi- culty in obtaining data, most LCA studies are limited to a few farms or models representing typical systems, especially in Latin America (Ruviaro et al., 2015; Dick et al., 2015; Mazzetto et al., 2015; Florindo et al., 2017). Furthermore, most studies focus on only one environmental impact category, usually GWP (McClelland et al., 2018).

The goal of this study was to evaluate multiple environmental burdens of dairy production, expanding the boundaries of LCA to account for coupled dairy-beef production systems and conse- quences for pure-beef farms. For the first time, we analysed a large number (552) farms, applying an expanded boundary approach to analyse the impact of milk and beef production from the whole cattle system, and considering five different impact categories (GWP, eutrophication potential (EP), acidification potential (AP), resource depletion potential (RDP) and LO).

8: The database used in this study was shared by the Costa Rican Ministry of Agriculture (MAG). It was based on information from the 2014 National Agricultural Census carried out by The National Institute of Statistics and Censuses, and financed by the World Bank and MAG (INEC, 2015). The survey was completed for 920 cattle farms and covered 390 factors relating to the farmer, farm char- acteristics, input costs and economic outputs. As an initial step, the dataset was cleaned, profiled, and validated, resulting in a dataset of 552 farms. The main reasons for excluding the farms from the dataset were i) lack of milk production data (for dairy farms); ii) no/ incomplete animal inventory; iii) lack of farm area (ha) data. The dataset was then split into farms that reported milk production (dairy cattle farms) and farms that did not report milk production (beef cattle farms) (Fig. 1).

The farms from the dairy sub-database were classified into five typologies previously defined by Vargas-Leitón et al. (2013), who used multivariate analysis to characterize and classify 1086 dairy herds of Dos Pinos, a Costa Rican dairy cooperative. We classified the surveyed farms based on the Key Performance Indicators (KPI) defined by Vargas-Leitón et al. (2013): i.e. altitude, stocking rate, percentage of specialised breeds, amount of concentrate feed fed to milking cows and total milk production (Supplementary Informa- tion - section S2 and Table S1). The KPI values (e.g. amount of concentrate per milking cow per day) were derived from relevant survey data. Farm altitude was not specified in the database, so we located each farm based on available coordinates (City, Region and others) using Google Earth and extracted the GPS information (latitude, longitude and altitude) for each farm using the "raster" package (Hijmans and van Etten, 2012) on R (R Core Team, 2018).

14: To generate the life cycle inventory, we extracted all the relevant activity data from the database, and applied algorithms for emis- sions of methane (CH4), nitrous oxide (N20), ammonia (NH3), car- bon dioxide (CO2) to air and N and P losses to water (Styles et al., 2018). All upstream burdens arising from the manufacture of fer- tilizer, production of concentrate feed, generation of electricity and supply of petrol/diesel were calculated using Ecoinvent v.3.5 (Wernet et al., 2016). Enteric fermentation CH4 and manure man- agement CH4 and N20 emissions were calculated using IPCC Tier 2 equations (IPCC, 2007). The digestibility and crude protein content of animal diets are critical determinants of enteric CH4 and N

excretion, respectively, based on IPCC Tier 2 calculations. These parameters were determined for each pasture genus reported, based on a recent review of the nutritional characteristics of Latin American pastures (Mazzetto et al., submitted). Field N20 emis- sions were calculated for nitrogen (N) excreted during grazing and N applied in form of manure and synthetic fertilizers using an IPCC Tier 1 approach. Indirect N20 emissions were based on NH3-N emissions and N leaching factors taken from national inventory reports (Duffy et al., 2014; Misselbrook et al., 2014). Greenhouse gas emissions were calculated as carbon dioxide (CO2) equivalents (COe), according to 100-year global warming potentials of 1, 25 and 298 per kg of CO2, CH4 and N20, respectively (IPCC, 2007). Where animals were imported into the farm, their upstream bur- dens associated with their rearing were included in the analysis as "bought" burdens. An average allocated burden per kg liveweight exported was calculated individually for each farm, which was treated as the "bought" burden and multiplied by the liveweight imported into the farm as replacement animals (i.e. farms were assumed to import beef from similar farms).

To calculate the LO footprint, we used the grazing area for the farms, the amount of concentrate fed to animals, and inferred up- stream footprints of all animals imported onto farms, including calves imported to beef fattening farms. The total land used for concentrate production was based on quantities of concentrate feed derived from concentrate feed costs reported for individual farms, the average composition of concentrate feed fed to cattle in Costa Rica and specific land occupation (m ² year-1 and GHG emissions arising from the production of each ingredient taken from Ecoin- vent v.3.5 (Wernet et al., 2016 - Supplementary Information, Table S2). The methods related to the other impact categories (EP, AP, RDP) are described in the Supplementary Information (section S4). All calculations were performed using MS Excel.
We applied attributional LCA to all farms individually in all of

the aforementioned typologies, to calculate environmental foot- prints per kg of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM), as defined by Gerber et al. (2011). Further detail on expanded boundary func- tional units (FU) is given later (sections 2.3 and 2.4). Farm-level results were averaged to generate mean values for each typology. A cradle to farm-gate scope was applied (Dairy System - Fig. 2). The impact categories analysed were global GWP (kgCOe) for a time horizon of 100 years, AP (kgSOe), EP (kgPOe), RDP (MJe) and LO (m² year (Supplementary Information- section 4, Table S3). Farm system burdens were allocated across milk and beef outputs based on respective gross energy content (ISO, 2006). We also calculated the footprint of beef produced on dairy farms (dairy- beef), and beef produced on suckler farms (for separated expanded boundary LCA calculations, described in sections 2.3 and 2.4), using a FU of one kg of beef after slaughter, assuming beef meat repre- sents an average 50% of animal live weight (Rodriguez et al., 2019; Machado et al., 2019).

18: <header>2.3. LCA - coupled dairy-fattening system (expanded boundaries approach) </header>

The farm-gate boundary approach described above takes into consideration the amount of beef produced by exported calves based on their live weight at the point of export from the dairy farm (assumed to be 40 kg immediately after birth). This approach dis- regards the potential for each exported calf to supply over ten times this live weight for beef production after fattening (Coupled Dairy- Fattening System - Fig. 2). In this study, we assume that female dairy calves, which are surplus to requirements, are exported to specialised beef farms (fattening dairy females typology), while male calves are exported to other specialised beef farms (fattening dairy males typology) (Fig. 2). Based on the performance of the relevant beef farm typologies within the MAG database, we calculated the average CO2e, SO2e, PO4e, energy (MJe) and land necessary for fattening 40 kg male and female calves up to a slaughter weight of 450 kg (Supplementary Information section 5 Table S4). According to the latest Costa Rican Agricultural census

(INEC, 2017), 96% of the beef produced in the country is pasture- based. We assumed that the fattening typologies (fattening dairy males, fattening dairy females and suckler beef typologies) do not use concentrate feed. The total beef produced, and burdens incurred for fattening these calves were added to the analysis above (section 2.4 creating a new "fattened calves" category). For this boundary expansion step, intermediate LCA results were calculated without allocation, considering all burdens associated with unique quantities of milk and dairy-beef produced by each coupled dairy- fattening system (Fig. 2).
19: <header>2.3. LCA - coupled dairy-fattening system (expanded boundaries approach) </header>

The farm-gate boundary approach described above takes into consideration the amount of beef produced by exported calves based on their live weight at the point of export from the dairy farm (assumed to be 40 kg immediately after birth). This approach dis- regards the potential for each exported calf to supply over ten times this live weight for beef production after fattening (Coupled Dairy- Fattening System - Fig. 2). In this study, we assume that female dairy calves, which are surplus to requirements, are exported to specialised beef farms (fattening dairy females typology), while male calves are exported to other specialised beef farms (fattening dairy males typology) (Fig. 2). Based on the performance of the relevant beef farm typologies within the MAG database, we calculated the average CO2e, SO2e, PO4e, energy (MJe) and land necessary for fattening 40 kg male and female calves up to a slaughter weight of 450 kg (Supplementary Information section 5 Table S4). According to the latest Costa Rican Agricultural census

(INEC, 2017), 96% of the beef produced in the country is pasture- based. We assumed that the fattening typologies (fattening dairy males, fattening dairy females and suckler beef typologies) do not use concentrate feed. The total beef produced, and burdens incurred for fattening these calves were added to the analysis above (section 2.4 creating a new "fattened calves" category). For this boundary expansion step, intermediate LCA results were calculated without allocation, considering all burdens associated with unique quantities of milk and dairy-beef produced by each coupled dairy- fattening system (Fig. 2).
20: Based on the total milk and dairy-beef production from the aforementioned approach, we calculated the beef:milk production ratio (as suggested by Vellinga and de Vries, 2018) for each coupled dairy-fattening farm system and compared this against the global demand ratio of beef to milk derived from global statistics (FAOSTAT, 2019 which is 0.10 kg beef per kg FPCM), and we assumed this also applied to consumption in Costa Rica. When coupled dairy-fattening systems produced less beef than this de- mand ratio, we assumed that the difference (deficit or "debit") must be produced by specialist suckler beef farms in Costa Rica (Fig. 3a). In order to calculate the footprint of suckler beef production in Costa Rica, we performed an attributional LCA on the 175 suckler beef farms available in the MAG database (Fig. 1). Average envi- ronmental burdens per kg beef obtained across these farms were then multiplied by the quantity of beef needed to make up the deficit between the dairy-beef production and the global demand ratio (Supplementary Information section S5 - Table S5). Where dairy-beef exceeded the global beef:milk demand ratio, we calcu- lated the difference and attributed a "credit" to these farms due to the avoidance of suckler beef production (Fig. 3b). This enabled environmental burdens to be calculated for a specific and constant FU considering both milk and beef production (as recommended by Vellinga and de Vries, 2018) of 1 kg milk FPCM + 0.10 kg beef. 23: From the original database of 920 farms, only 552 (60%) con- tained sufficient data for performing the LCA after the data cleaning process (section 2.1). In this study, the average dairy farm in Costa Rica had 25 ha of grazing area and applied 50 kg N ha year The average farm had 24 milking cows, produced 12 kg milk cow-1day-1, with 4 kg of concentrate given to each cow daily. The typologies with the more intensive production (SD_I_U and SD_I_L) had the greater number of cows, higher N fertiliser application, higher milk production and less grazing area (Table 1), while the DPTB had the higher number of animals exported, especially

animals other than calves (Table 1). The specialised dairy typologies exported greater numbers of male calves. Other details (animal inventory, number of animals bought and sold) can be found in the Supplementary Material (section S7, Tables S6 to S8). SD_E_L: Specialised Dairy Extensive in the Lowlands; SD_I_L: Specialised Dairy Intensive in the Lowlands; SD_SI_U: Specialised Dairy Semi-Intensive in the Uplands; SD_I_U: Specialised Dairy Intensive in the Uplands; DP: Dual Purpose; DPTD: Dual Purpose Towards Dairy; DPTB: Dual Purpose Towards Beef. We calculated the normalised emission/energy/land per farm typology, normalising burdens expressed for the various FUs against average annual per capita burdens (CML, 2016). Land occupation and GWP had the highest normalised scores for dairy and beef production in Costa Rica, whilst RDP had the lowest nor- malised scores (Fig. 4). Accordingly, we choosed to focus on results for GWP and LO. Table 2 summarises the final footprint results for EP, AP and RDP. All the results for the other steps are represented as Tables in the Supplementary Information (Tables S9 to S13).

4: The Dual Purpose (DP) and Dual Purpose Towards Dairy (DPTD) farms had the largest GWP footprints for milk and dairy-beef, while the smallest footprints were found for the SD_I_U typology (Fig. 5). Allocated beef footprints produced by the dairy farms followed the same pattern as allocated milk footprints across typologies (Fig. 5). A partial Costa Rican carbon footprint was undertaken by Wattiaux et al. (2016), indicating a range of 0.38-1.02 kg CO2e kg-1 FPCM. The milk GWP footprint for SD_I_U is in line with other studies in temperate climates (Castanheira et al., 2010; Schader et al., 2014; Penati et al., 2013; Kiefer et al., 2015; Salvador et al., 2017; Guerci et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2018; Pirlo and Lolli, 2019), and the other footprints are within the range suggested by Asselin-Balençon et al.
(2013) and Del Prado et al. (2013) (0.74-2.46 kg CO2e kg-1 FPCM), except DP, DPTD and SD_SI_U (Fig. 5).

The comparatively small farm-gate milk GWP footprint for the SD_I_U typology (Fig. 5) is mainly the result of higher cow pro- ductivity compared with other typologies (Table 1) and relatively low emissions from housing and manure management in Costa Rica owing to cows grazing outdoors throughout the year. Knapp et al. (2014) showed that milk productivity gains outweighed increases in enteric CH4 emission due to higher dry matter intake, leading to a smaller footprint per unit of product for more productive animals. Previous studies have shown a strong inverse correlation between carbon footprint and milk yield per animal (e.g. Gerber et al., 2011). Enteric fermentation was the main source of GHG for all ty- pologies (Fig. 5), as typically found in previous milk and beef

GHG emissions from manure manage- ment were extremely low, reflecting the dominance of year-round grazing in the region (Fig. 5). Environmental footprints were higher for beef from the beef-only farms than from dairy farms (except for the DP, DPTD and SD_SI_U typologies - Fig. 5) as also noted by Vellinga and de Vries (2018), de Vries et al. 5: The Dual Purpose (DP) and Dual Purpose Towards Dairy (DPTD) farms had the largest GWP footprints for milk and dairy-beef, while the smallest footprints were found for the SD_I_U typology (Fig. 5). Allocated beef footprints produced by the dairy farms followed the same pattern as allocated milk footprints across typologies (Fig. 5). A partial Costa Rican carbon footprint was undertaken by Wattiaux et al. (2016), indicating a range of 0.38-1.02 kg CO2e kg-1 FPCM. The milk GWP footprint for SD_I_U is in line with other studies in temperate climates (Castanheira et al., 2010; Schader et al., 2014; Penati et al., 2013; Kiefer et al., 2015; Salvador et al., 2017; Guerci et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2018; Pirlo and Lolli, 2019), and the other footprints are within the range suggested by Asselin-Balençon et al.
(2013) and Del Prado et al. (2013) (0.74-2.46 kg CO2e kg-1 FPCM), except DP, DPTD and SD_SI_U (Fig. 5).

The comparatively small farm-gate milk GWP footprint for the SD_I_U typology (Fig. 5) is mainly the result of higher cow pro- ductivity compared with other typologies (Table 1) and relatively low emissions from housing and manure management in Costa Rica owing to cows grazing outdoors throughout the year. Knapp et al. (2014) showed that milk productivity gains outweighed increases in enteric CH4 emission due to higher dry matter intake, leading to a smaller footprint per unit of product for more productive animals. Previous studies have shown a strong inverse correlation between carbon footprint and milk yield per animal (e.g. Gerber et al., 2011). Enteric fermentation was the main source of GHG for all ty- pologies (Fig. 5), as typically found in previous milk and beef

GHG emissions from manure manage- ment were extremely low, reflecting the dominance of year-round grazing in the region (Fig. 5). Environmental footprints were higher for beef from the beef-only farms than from dairy farms (except for the DP, DPTD and SD_SI_U typologies - Fig. 5) as also noted by Vellinga and de Vries (2018), de Vries et al. (2015) and Nguyen et al. (2010). This is mainly a result of the need for dedicated suckler cows to rear beef calves on pure beef farms. 9: The Dual Purpose (DP) and Dual Purpose Towards Dairy (DPTD) farms had the largest GWP footprints for milk and dairy-beef, while the smallest footprints were found for the SD_I_U typology (Fig. 5). Allocated beef footprints produced by the dairy farms followed the same pattern as allocated milk footprints across typologies (Fig. 5). A partial Costa Rican carbon footprint was undertaken by Wattiaux et al. (2016), indicating a range of 0.38-1.02 kg CO2e kg-1 FPCM. The milk GWP footprint for SD_I_U is in line with other studies in temperate climates (Castanheira et al., 2010; Schader et al., 2014; Penati et al., 2013; Kiefer et al., 2015; Salvador et al., 2017; Guerci et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2018; Pirlo and Lolli, 2019), and the other footprints are within the range suggested by Asselin-Balençon et al.
(2013) and Del Prado et al. (2013) (0.74-2.46 kg CO2e kg-1 FPCM), except DP, DPTD and SD_SI_U (Fig. 5).

The comparatively small farm-gate milk GWP footprint for the SD_I_U typology (Fig. 5) is mainly the result of higher cow pro- ductivity compared with other typologies (Table 1) and relatively low emissions from housing and manure management in Costa Rica owing to cows grazing outdoors throughout the year. Knapp et al. (2014) showed that milk productivity gains outweighed increases in enteric CH4 emission due to higher dry matter intake, leading to a smaller footprint per unit of product for more productive animals. Previous studies have shown a strong inverse correlation between carbon footprint and milk yield per animal (e.g. Gerber et al., 2011). Enteric fermentation was the main source of GHG for all ty- pologies (Fig. 5), as typically found in previous milk and beef

13: <header>3.3. Coupled dairy-fattening system level of analysis </header>

The DPTB farms produced more beef per kg of milk than the other typologies (Fig. 6). After expanding the boundaries (Coupled Dairy-Fattening System), farms across four typologies produced more than the 0.10 ratio on average. Ratios are relatively high owing to low milk production per cow compared with dairy sys- tems in industrialised countries (Vellinga and de Vries, 2018). The dual purpose typologies (DPTB and DP) showed a high beef: milk ratio at both farm-gate and expanded boundary level, while the specialised dairy typologies (SD_SI_U and SD_I_L) only reached higher beef ratios after expanding the boundaries to consider beef produced by fattened calves (Fig. 6).

The DPTB showed a lower GWP milk&beef footprint (Fig. 7) due to the large beef:milk ratio (Fig. 6), generating a "credit" that reduced the milk&beef footprint to a smaller size than the other specialised dairy farms (Fig. 7). Other typologies, including DP and SD_SI_U also received "credits" when the boundaries were expanded (Fig. 7). Meanwhile, other typologies incurred "debits" that were added to the milk&beef footprint, owing to the need for additional suckler-beef production to compensate for a dairy-beef shortfall relative to final beef demand (Fig. 7). At the farm-gate, the amount of beef exported by the more intensive typologies was low (Table 1) because most of the animals exported were pre-weaned calves, resulting in a small beef:milk ratio (Fig. 6). When the boundaries were expanded to account for downstream fattening of these calves, beef production from these specialised dairy farms became similar to the dual purpose farms at the farm level (Table 1), reducing the "debit" to be paid by the suckler beef farms (Figs. 5 and 6). However, due to the high yield of milk per cow, the beef:milk ratio was still lower than the dual purpose farms where the herd structure was oriented more to the beef production (DPTB - Fig. 6). The main factor that explained our milk&beef carbon footprint (Fig. 7) is the balance between herd structure and milk production. The influence of the herd structure is noted on the dual purpose farms studied, which were more diverse and showed a wider range of performance (Fig. 8). All dual purpose typologies (DP, DPTB and DPTD) had similar milk pro- duction per cow (Table 1), but the DPTD typology had more milking

SD_E_L: Specialised Dairy Extensive in the Lowlands; SD_I_L: Specialised Dairy Intensive in the Lowlands; SD_SI_U: Specialised Dairy Semi-Intensive in the Uplands; SD_I_U: Specialised Dairy Intensive in the Uplands; DP: Dual Purpose; DPTD: Dual Purpose Towards Dairy; DPTB: Dual Purpose Towards Beef.

cows and exported fewer animals (Table 1). As a consequence, the beef:milk ratio of DPTD is smaller than for the other dual purpose typologies (Fig. 6), generating debits added to the milk&beef footprint (Fig. 7).

Large LO "credits" were also generated by the higher beef:milk ratio for DPTB farms, compared with smaller LO credits for DP and SD_SI_U farms (Coupled Dairy-Fattening System) (Fig. 9). However, despite these smaller credits, the most intensive typologies (SD_I_U and SD_I_L) still resulted in smallest overall LO for the milk&beef footprint (Fig. 9). LO results displayed a different pattern to GWP footprints, reflecting the efficiency (intensity) of land used. The specialised dairy farms had a smaller total grazing area, especially the more intensive typologies (SD_I_L and SD_I_U - Table 1). The land occupation is especially important in Costa Rica, because their carbon-neutral plan relies on the reforestation of the country (Flagg, 2018). The most recent FAO available dataset (FAOSTAT, 2019) shows that Costa Rica increased its forested area from 25% to 55% of national land area between 2000 and 2016. In order to comply with its environmental plan, the trade-off between farm area (Fig. 9) and GWP footprint (Fig. 7) must be considered. This trade-off illustrates the importance of considering more than one impact category when analysing the impact of milk and beef pro- duction. From 2000 to 2016, 173 LCA studies dealing with the environmental impact of livestock production were published (McClelland et al., 2018) and 28% (50 studies) analysed only the GWP impact. The number of multi-categories LCA studies has increased over recent years, identifying synergies and trade-offs among systems and management practices (McClelland et al., 2018).

Vellinga and de Vries (2018), Styles et al. (2018) and Soteriades et al. (2019) also worked on expanding the boundaries of the dairy system, showing that an integrated approach is necessary when evaluating the dairy sector that is closely coupled with beef pro- duction. For all the typologies, the expanded boundary LCA of milk plus beef production indicated lower footprints when the bound- aries were expanded (coupled dairy-beef system) (Figs. 7 and 8). This is because of the extra beef being produced by the fattened calves (Fig. 6) which outweighs the extra emission/land necessary during the fattening process, reducing the final milk&beef footprint (Figs. 6 and 8).

This is because of the extra beef being produced by the fattened calves (Fig. 6) which outweighs the extra emission/land necessary during the fattening process, reducing the final milk&beef footprint (Figs. 6 and 8).
18: The purpose of LCA is not just to account for environmental burdens of a product, but also to identify possibilities for optimi- sation and mitigation within production systems. Common prac- tices that are performed in the more intensive dairy production to reduce the impact at farm-gate level could have counter-productive consequences for coupled beef production systems captured by expanded boundary analysis. One example is killing male dairy calves due to the small economic incentive for fattening these an- imals, which typically gain weight more slowly than dedicated beef breeds. Killing surplus calves may appear environmentally and economically efficient from isolated analysis of dairy and beef systems: dairy systems minimise inputs and emissions associated with calf maintenance whilst specialising on feed conversion effi- ciency into milk; increased productivity from import of dedicated beef breeds into beef fattening systems reduces the impact per kg

Land occupation footprint summing the milk and beef from dairy farms (Milk + Dairy Beef - blue bars) and the deficit/credit (Beef Deficit - red bars) footprints, for all the typologies considering the Dairy System and the Coupled Dairy-Fattening System. The green bars represent the final footprint (i.e. the sum of the two previous footprints). SD_E_L: Specialised Dairy Extensive in the Lowlands; SD_I_L: Specialised Dairy Intensive in the Lowlands; SD_SI_U: Specialised Dairy Semi-Intensive in the Uplands; SD_I_U: Specialised Dairy Intensive in the Uplands; DP: Dual Purpose; DPTD: Dual Purpose Towards Dairy; DPTB: Dual Purpose Towards Beef. The error bars represent the 95% confidence interval. The 0.XX denotes the beef:milk ratio for each individual farm. beef (excluding breeding emissions that typically arise on separate beef breeding farms). But, when calculating the environmental impact at the Coupled-Dairy System level, it is clear that overall GWP and LO footprints increase significantly if dairy calves are killed immediately after birth, because more beef suckler calves then need to be produced (Fig. 10).

Thus, it is clear that expanding the boundaries of analysis when assessing sustainable intensification and climate mitigation stra- tegies for dairy systems, to consider implications for inter- connected beef production systems, is likely to change conclusions on appropriate pathways and specific management practises. For example, rather than being culled, less-productive milking cows could be crossed with pedigree beef bulls to increase dairy-beef production and reduce overall impacts from beef production by displacing less efficient suckler-beef production (Fig. 5). Alterna- tively, retaining cows for fewer lactations would result in more beef emanating from culled milking cows (but also fewer calves avail- able for fattening). Meanwhile, beef system consequences of dairy system modifications are critically dependent upon the relative (in) efficiency of suckler beef production. The environmental impact of beef production in Europe is, on average, lower than in Latin America (Desjardins et al., 2012) because most of the European beef is derived from dairy cows. Sustainable intensification of beef production would result in a smaller final footprint for the whole cattle system and may even change the direction of consequences arising from dairy system specialisation. Reducing the footprint of beef production can be achieved through multiple steps of

intensification, with mitigation potential in the range of 2%-57% demonstrated for Latin American beef (Mazzetto et al., 2015). There is an urgent need to reassess the plethora of mitigation options for dairy and beef systems in the more holistic context of inter- connected cattle systems, using an expanded boundary LCA approach.

We recognise some of the limitations/uncertainties of this study. The large confidence intervals reported for typology footprints reflect the diversity of farms classified within each typology grouping, but may also reflect some underlying uncertainties within the database used. As the farm inventory data consist of a snapshot of each farm at the time that the survey was undertaken, they may not fully represent the average situation over one year of farm operation. The loss of 40% of sample farms owing to data gaps indicates patchy quality of data collection in Costa Rica. Data collection and validation techniques for this Costa Rican survey could be further developed towards the statistical representative- ness and verified book-keeping approach used in e.g. the European Farm Accountancy Data Network (EC, 2019). Enhanced data collection would improve the accuracy and usefulness of future studies.

Specialised dairy farms produced milk with the smallest carbon and land footprints, but dual purpose dairy-beef farms resulted in lower overall greenhouse gas emissions for the delivery of one kg milk plus 100 g beef (the global average demand for these commodities). We advocate the application of expanded boundary LCA to evaluate the environmental efficiency of com- bined milk and beef production from interconnected dairy, dairy- beef fattening and suckler-beef systems (or fully consequential LCA to assess changes in these systems), in order to correctly identify effective mitigation practices and/or design new sustain- able intensification strategies that minimise the risk of unintended "emissions leakage". Such strategies must account for complex animal exchanges (especially replacement heifers and fattening animals) among dairy and beef farms. Furthermore, the use of the beef:milk as a functional unit can help in understanding the po- tential change of the environmental burdens due to the change in demand (e.g., increasing the consumption of plant-based milk al- ternatives means less cattle milk being produced, reducing the footprint of milk, but increasing the footprint of beef - due to the inherent connection between milk and beef).

Our results also highlight the importance of analysing multiple environmental impact categories and imply that there is no single strategy likely to simultaneously address all environmental chal- lenges facing the dairy sector. Land occupation will have additional consequences for greenhouse gas emissions given the importance of land use, land use change and forestry for the global greenhouse gas budget (IPCC, 2007). It is possible that the higher carbon

footprint of combined milk and beef production for specialised dairy farms could be offset by CO2 sequestration via afforestation on land spared from cattle production relative to other farm ty- pologies or agroforestry (Brook et al., in review). Further research needs to consider feasible combinations of dairy and beef farm typologies at a regional level that minimise emissions and use land efficiently in order to generate a full picture of the net environ- mental efficiency of alternative sustainable intensification strate- gies at national and international scales.
